        "path": "docs/ref/contrib/postgres/lookups.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 1,
        "deleted": 1,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " MariaDB notes\n =============\n \n-Django supports MariaDB 10.6 and higher.\n+Django supports MariaDB 10.11 and higher.\n \n To use MariaDB, use the MySQL backend, which is shared between the two. See the\n :ref:`MySQL notes <mysql-notes>` for more details.\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 421
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 421
            },
            "section": "non-durable <https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/non-durability.html>`_."
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/ref/databases.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 5,
        "deleted": 5,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " \n     Built-in database functions (such as\n     :class:`~django.db.models.functions.Cast`) vary in whether arguments such\n-    as ``output_field`` can be supplied positionally or only by keyword. For\n-    ``output_field`` and several other cases, the input ultimately reaches\n-    ``Func()`` as a keyword argument, so the advice to avoid constructing\n-    keyword arguments from untrusted user input applies as equally to these\n-    arguments as it does to ``**extra``.\n+    as ``output_field`` can be supplied as positional arguments or only by\n+    keyword. For ``output_field`` and several other cases, the input ultimately\n+    reaches ``Func()`` as a keyword argument, so the advice to avoid\n+    constructing keyword arguments from untrusted user input applies as equally\n+    to these arguments as it does to ``**extra``.\n \n ``Aggregate()`` expressions\n ---------------------------\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 11,
              "startLine": 438
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 11,
              "startLine": 438
            },
            "section": "return type."
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/ref/models/expressions.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 5,
        "deleted": 5,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " .. class:: UUIDField(**options)\n \n A field for storing universally unique identifiers. Uses Python's\n-:class:`~python:uuid.UUID` class. When used on PostgreSQL and MariaDB 10.7+,\n-this stores in a ``uuid`` datatype, otherwise in a ``char(32)``.\n+:class:`~python:uuid.UUID` class. When used on PostgreSQL and MariaDB, this\n+stores in a ``uuid`` datatype, otherwise in a ``char(32)``.\n \n Universally unique identifiers are a good alternative to :class:`AutoField` for\n :attr:`~Field.primary_key`. The database will not generate the UUID for you, so\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 8,
              "startLine": 1649
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 8,
              "startLine": 1649
            },
            "section": "Like all :class:`CharField` subclasses, :class:`URLField` takes the optional"
          },
          {
            "body": " Note that a callable (with the parentheses omitted) is passed to ``default``,\n not an instance of ``UUID``.\n \n-.. admonition:: Lookups on PostgreSQL and MariaDB 10.7+\n+.. admonition:: Lookups on PostgreSQL and MariaDB\n \n     Using :lookup:`iexact`, :lookup:`contains`, :lookup:`icontains`,\n     :lookup:`startswith`, :lookup:`istartswith`, :lookup:`endswith`, or\n     :lookup:`iendswith` lookups on PostgreSQL don't work for values without\n-    hyphens, because PostgreSQL and MariaDB 10.7+ store them in a hyphenated\n-    uuid datatype type.\n+    hyphens, because PostgreSQL and MariaDB store them in a hyphenated uuid\n+    datatype type.\n \n .. _relationship-fields:\n \n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 13,
              "startLine": 1667
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 13,
              "startLine": 1667
            },
            "section": "it is recommended to use :attr:`~Field.default`::"
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/ref/models/fields.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 0,
        "deleted": 5,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " For example ``Index(fields=['headline', '-pub_date'])`` would create SQL with\n ``(headline, pub_date DESC)``.\n \n-.. admonition:: MariaDB\n-\n-    Index ordering isn't supported on MariaDB < 10.8. In that case, a\n-    descending index is created as a normal index.\n-\n ``name``\n --------\n \n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 6,
              "startLine": 81
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 11,
              "startLine": 81
            },
            "section": "field's name."
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/ref/models/indexes.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 17,
        "deleted": 23,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": "     >>> blogs = Blog.objects.alias(entries=Count(\"entry\")).filter(entries__gt=5)\n \n ``alias()`` can be used in conjunction with :meth:`annotate`, :meth:`exclude`,\n-:meth:`filter`, :meth:`order_by`, and :meth:`update`. To use aliased expression\n-with other methods (e.g. :meth:`aggregate`), you must promote it to an\n-annotation::\n+:meth:`filter`, :meth:`order_by`, and :meth:`update`. To use an aliased\n+expression with other methods (e.g. :meth:`aggregate`), you must promote it to\n+an annotation::\n \n     Blog.objects.alias(entries=Count(\"entry\")).annotate(\n         entries=F(\"entries\"),\n     ).aggregate(Sum(\"entries\"))\n \n :meth:`filter` and :meth:`order_by` can take expressions directly, but\n expression construction and usage often does not happen in the same place (for\n example, ``QuerySet`` method creates expressions, for later use in views).\n-``alias()`` allows building complex expressions incrementally, possibly\n-spanning multiple methods and modules, refer to the expression parts by their\n-aliases and only use :meth:`annotate` for the final result.\n+``alias()`` allows building complex expressions incrementally (possibly\n+spanning multiple methods and modules), referring to the expression parts by\n+their aliases, and only using :meth:`annotate` for the final result.\n \n ``order_by()``\n ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 20,
              "startLine": 330
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 20,
              "startLine": 330
            },
            "section": "interested in the exact number of entries, you could do this:"
          },
          {
            "body": " .. code-block:: pycon\n \n     >>> Author.objects.distinct()\n-    [...]\n-\n+    <QuerySet [...]>\n     >>> Entry.objects.order_by(\"pub_date\").distinct(\"pub_date\")\n-    [...]\n-\n+    <QuerySet [...]>\n     >>> Entry.objects.order_by(\"blog\").distinct(\"blog\")\n-    [...]\n-\n+    <QuerySet [...]>\n     >>> Entry.objects.order_by(\"author\", \"pub_date\").distinct(\"author\", \"pub_date\")\n-    [...]\n-\n+    <QuerySet [...]>\n     >>> Entry.objects.order_by(\"blog__name\", \"mod_date\").distinct(\"blog__name\", \"mod_date\")\n-    [...]\n-\n+    <QuerySet [...]>\n     >>> Entry.objects.order_by(\"author\", \"pub_date\").distinct(\"author\")\n-    [...]\n+    <QuerySet [...]>\n \n .. note::\n     Keep in mind that :meth:`order_by` uses any default related model ordering\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 17,
              "startLine": 549
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 22,
              "startLine": 549
            },
            "section": "Examples (those after the first will only work on PostgreSQL):"
          },
          {
            "body": " .. code-block:: pycon\n \n     >>> Pizza.objects.all()\n-    [\"Hawaiian (ham, pineapple)\", \"Seafood (prawns, smoked salmon)\"...\n+    <QuerySet [<Pizza: Hawaiian (ham, pineapple)>, <Pizza: Seafood (prawns, smoked salmon)>, ...]>\n \n The problem with this is that every time ``Pizza.__str__()`` asks for\n ``self.toppings.all()`` it has to query the database, so\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 1199
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 1204
            },
            "section": "and run:"
          },
          {
            "body": "     <QuerySet [<Person: ...>, ...]>\n \n The ``postgresql``, ``oracle``, and ``mysql`` database backends support\n-``select_for_update()``. However, MariaDB only supports the ``nowait``\n-argument, MariaDB 10.6+ also supports the ``skip_locked`` argument, and MySQL\n-supports the ``nowait``, ``skip_locked``, and ``of`` arguments. The ``no_key``\n-argument is only supported on PostgreSQL.\n+``select_for_update()``. However, MariaDB only supports the ``nowait`` and\n+``skip_locked`` arguments, and MySQL supports the ``nowait``, ``skip_locked``,\n+and ``of`` arguments. The ``no_key`` argument is only supported on PostgreSQL.\n \n Passing ``nowait=True``, ``skip_locked=True``, ``no_key=True``, or ``of`` to\n ``select_for_update()`` using database backends that do not support these\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 9,
              "startLine": 1988
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 10,
              "startLine": 1993
            },
            "section": "them:"
          },
          {
            "body": " \n     >>> prefetch = Prefetch(\"choice_set\", queryset=voted_choices, to_attr=\"voted_choices\")\n     >>> Question.objects.prefetch_related(prefetch).get().voted_choices\n-    [<Choice: The sky>]\n+    <QuerySet [<Choice: The sky>]>\n     >>> Question.objects.prefetch_related(prefetch).get().choice_set.all()\n     <QuerySet [<Choice: Not much>, <Choice: The sky>, <Choice: Just hacking again>]>\n \n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 4234
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 4240
            },
            "section": "attribute:"
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/ref/models/querysets.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 9,
        "deleted": 0,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": "+===========================\n+Django 4.2.28 release notes\n+===========================\n+\n+*February 3, 2026*\n+\n+Django 4.2.28 fixes three security issues with severity \"high\", two security\n+issues with severity \"moderate\", and one security issue with severity \"low\" in\n+4.2.27.\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 9,
              "startLine": 1
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 0,
              "startLine": 0
            }
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/releases/4.2.28.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 4,
        "deleted": 7,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " Django 5.2.11 release notes\n ===========================\n \n-*Expected February 3, 2026*\n+*February 3, 2026*\n \n-Django 5.2.11 fixes several bugs in 5.2.10.\n-\n-Bugfixes\n-========\n-\n-* ...\n+Django 5.2.11 fixes three security issues with severity \"high\", two security\n+issues with severity \"moderate\", and one security issue with severity \"low\" in\n+5.2.10.\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 8,
              "startLine": 2
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 11,
              "startLine": 2
            }
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/releases/5.2.11.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 11,
        "deleted": 3,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " Django 6.0.2 release notes\n ==========================\n \n-*Expected February 3, 2026*\n+*February 3, 2026*\n \n-Django 6.0.2 fixes several bugs in 6.0.1.\n+Django 6.0.2 fixes three security issues with severity \"high\", two security\n+issues with severity \"moderate\", one security issue with severity \"low\", and\n+several bugs in 6.0.1.\n \n Bugfixes\n ========\n \n-* ...\n+* Fixed a visual regression in Django 6.0 that caused the admin filter sidebar\n+  to wrap below the changelist when filter elements contained long text\n+  (:ticket:`36850`).\n+\n+* Fixed a regression in Django 6.0 where ``auto_now_add`` field values were not\n+  populated during ``INSERT`` operations, due to incorrect parameters passed to\n+  ``field.pre_save()`` (:ticket:`36847`).\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 19,
              "startLine": 2
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 11,
              "startLine": 2
            }
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/releases/6.0.2.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 12,
        "deleted": 2,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " * The admin site login view now redirects authenticated users to the next URL,\n   if available, instead of always redirecting to the admin index page.\n \n+* The admin's ``FilteredSelectMultiple`` widget now uses ``<optgroup>``\\s to\n+  preserve :ref:`named groups <field-choices-named-groups>` (e.g.\n+  ``choices=[(\"Group\", [(\"1\", \"Item\")]), ...]``).\n+\n :mod:`django.contrib.admindocs`\n ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n \n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 10,
              "startLine": 95
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 6,
              "startLine": 95
            },
            "section": "Minor features"
          },
          {
            "body": " \n * Management commands now set :class:`~argparse.ArgumentParser`\\'s\n   ``suggest_on_error`` argument to ``True`` by default on Python 3.14, enabling\n-  suggestions for mistyped subcommand names and argument choices.\n+  suggestions for incorrectly typed subcommand names and argument choices.\n \n * The :djadmin:`loaddata` command now calls\n   :data:`~django.db.models.signals.m2m_changed` signals with ``raw=True`` when\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 242
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 238
            },
            "section": "Management Commands"
          },
          {
            "body": " ~~~~~~~~~\n \n * :func:`~django.utils.dateparse.parse_duration` now supports ISO 8601\n-  durations expressed in weeks (``PnW``).\n+  time periods expressed in weeks (``PnW``).\n \n Validators\n ~~~~~~~~~~\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 340
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 336
            },
            "section": "Utilities"
          },
          {
            "body": " Upstream support for MySQL 8.0 ends in April 2026, and MySQL 8.1-8.3 are\n short-term innovation releases. Django 6.1 supports MySQL 8.4 and higher.\n \n+Dropped support for MariaDB < 10.11\n+-----------------------------------\n+\n+Upstream support for MariaDB 10.6 ends in July 2026, and MariaDB 10.7-10.10 are\n+short-term maintenance releases. Django 6.1 supports MariaDB 10.11 and higher.\n+\n Miscellaneous\n -------------\n \n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 12,
              "startLine": 403
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 6,
              "startLine": 399
            },
            "section": "Dropped support for MySQL < 8.4"
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/releases/6.1.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 1,
        "deleted": 0,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " .. toctree::\n    :maxdepth: 1\n \n+   4.2.28\n    4.2.27\n    4.2.26\n    4.2.25\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 103
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 6,
              "startLine": 103
            },
            "section": "versions of the documentation contain the release notes for any later releases."
          }
        ],

      },

      },
      {
        "added": 2,
        "deleted": 2,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " \n .. note::\n \n-    Django uses ``asgiref.sync.iscoroutinefunction`` to test if your view is\n+    Django uses ``inspect.iscoroutinefunction`` to test if your view is\n     asynchronous or not. If you implement your own method of returning a\n-    coroutine, ensure you use ``asgiref.sync.markcoroutinefunction`` so this\n+    coroutine, ensure you use ``inspect.markcoroutinefunction`` so this\n     function returns ``True``.\n \n Under a WSGI server, async views will run in their own, one-off event loop.\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 9,
              "startLine": 28
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 9,
              "startLine": 28
            },
            "section": "class-based view, this means declaring the HTTP method handlers, such as"
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/topics/async.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 3,
        "deleted": 3,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": " ``async_capable = True``, then Django will pass it the request without\n converting it. In this case, you can work out if your middleware will receive\n async requests by checking if the ``get_response`` object you are passed is a\n-coroutine function, using ``asgiref.sync.iscoroutinefunction``.\n+coroutine function, using ``inspect.iscoroutinefunction``.\n \n The ``django.utils.decorators`` module contains\n :func:`~django.utils.decorators.sync_only_middleware`,\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 318
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 318
            },
            "section": "If your middleware has both ``sync_capable = True`` and"
          },
          {
            "body": " \n Here's an example of how to create a middleware function that supports both::\n \n-    from asgiref.sync import iscoroutinefunction\n+    from inspect import iscoroutinefunction\n     from django.utils.decorators import sync_and_async_middleware\n \n \n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 337
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 337
            },
            "section": "at an additional performance penalty."
          },
          {
            "body": " When using an asynchronous class-based middleware, you must ensure that\n instances are correctly marked as coroutine functions::\n \n-    from asgiref.sync import iscoroutinefunction, markcoroutinefunction\n+    from inspect import iscoroutinefunction, markcoroutinefunction\n \n \n     class AsyncMiddleware:\n",
            "newRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 373
            },
            "oldRange": {
              "lines": 7,
              "startLine": 373
            },
            "section": "Here's an example of how to create a middleware function that supports both::"
          }
        ],
        "path": "docs/topics/http/middleware.txt"
      },
      {
        "added": 160,
        "deleted": 0,
        "hunks": [
          {
            "body": "     assert.equal(toSelectBox.options.length, selectedOptions.length);\n     assert.notEqual(fromSelectBox.scrollTop, 0);\n });\n+\n+QUnit.test('retain optgroups', function(assert) {\n+    const $ = django.jQuery;\n+    $('<select id=\"id\"></select>').appendTo('#qunit-fixture');\n+    const grp = $('<optgroup label=\"group one\">').appendTo('#id');\n+    $('<option value=\"0\">A</option>').appendTo(grp);\n+    $('</optgroup>').appendTo('#id');\n+    $('<option value=\"1\">B</option>').appendTo('#id');\n+    SelectBox.init('id');\n+    SelectBox.redisplay('id');\n+    assert.equal($('#id option').length, 2);\n+    assert.equal($('#id optgroup').length, 1);\n+});\n+\n+QUnit.test('sort optgroups', function(assert) {\n+    const $ = django.jQuery;\n+    $('<select id=\"id\"></select>').appendTo('#qunit-fixture');\n+    // Add optgroups in non-alphabetical order\n+    const grp2 = $('<optgroup label=\"Group B\">').appendTo('#id');\n+    $('<option value=\"3\">Item 3</option>').appendTo(grp2);\n+    $('<option value=\"4\">Item 4</option>').appendTo(grp2);\n+    const grp1 = $('<optgroup label=\"Group A\">').appendTo('#id');\n+    $('<option value=\"1\">Item 1</option>').appendTo(grp1);\n+    $('<option value=\"2\">Item 2</option>').appendTo(grp1);\n+\n+    SelectBox.init('id');\n+\n+    // Verify cache is sorted by group then by item\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id.length, 4);\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[0].group, 'Group A');\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[0].text, 'Item 1');\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[1].group, 'Group A');\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[1].text, 'Item 2');\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[2].group, 'Group B');\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[2].text, 'Item 3');\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[3].group, 'Group B');\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[3].text, 'Item 4');\n+});\n+\n+QUnit.test('do not SelectBox.init('id');\n+\n+    // Verify cache preserves original order (not sorted)\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id.length, 3);\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[0].text, 'Zebra');\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[1].text, 'Apple');\n+    assert.equal(SelectBox.cache.id[2].text, 'Banana');\n+});\n+\n+QUnit.test('move with optgroups sorts', function(assert) {\n+    const $ =             "body": "+import sys\n+from pathlib import Path\n+\n+\n+def main():\n+    repo_root = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent\n+    sys.path[:0] = [str(repo_root / \"tests\"), str(repo_root)]\n+\n+    from runtests import ALWAYS_INSTALLED_APPS, get_apps_to_install, get_test_modules\n+\n+    import django\n+    from django.apps import apps\n+    from django.core.management import call_command\n+\n+    django.setup()\n+\n+    test_modules = list(get_test_modules(gis_enabled=False))\n+    installed_apps = list(ALWAYS_INSTALLED_APPS)\n+    for app in get_apps_to_install(test_modules):\n+        # Check against the list to prevent duplicate errors.\n+        if app not in installed_apps:\n+            installed_apps.append(app)\n+    apps.set_installed_apps(installed_apps)\n+\n+    # Note: We don't use check=True here because --check calls sys.exit(1)\n+    # instead of raising CommandError when migrations are missing.\n+    call_command(\"makemigrations\", \"--check\", verbosity=3)\n+\n+\n+if __name__ == \"__main__\":\n+    main()\n",

            "body": "         cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n         self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [abcd])\n \n+    def test_exact_lookup_with_invalid_value(self):\n+        Child.objects.create(name=\"Test\", age=10)\n+        m = admin.ModelAdmin(Child, custom_site)\n+        m.search_fields = [\"pk__exact\"]\n+\n+        request = self.factory.get(\"/\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \"foo\"})\n+        request.user = self.superuser\n+\n+        # Invalid values are gracefully ignored.\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [])\n+\n+    def test_exact_lookup_mixed_terms(self):\n+        \"\"\"\n+        Multi-term search validates each term independently.\n+\n+        For 'foo 123' with search_fields=['name__icontains', 'age__exact']:\n+        - 'foo': age lookup skipped (invalid), name lookup used\n+        - '123': both lookups used (valid for age)\n+        No Cast should be used; invalid lookups are simply skipped.\n+        \"\"\"\n+        child = Child.objects.create(name=\"foo123\", age=123)\n+        Child.objects.create(name=\"other\", age=456)\n+        m = admin.ModelAdmin(Child, custom_site)\n+        m.search_fields = [\"name__icontains\", \"age__exact\"]\n+\n+        request = self.factory.get(\"/\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \"foo 123\"})\n+        request.user = self.superuser\n+\n+        # One result matching on foo and 123.\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [child])\n+\n+        # \"xyz\" - invalid for age (skipped), no match for name either.\n+        request = self.factory.get(\"/\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \"xyz\"})\n+        request.user = self.superuser\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [])\n+\n+    def test_exact_lookup_with_more_lenient_formfield(self):\n+        \"\"\"\n+        Exact lookups on BooleanField use formfield().to_python() for lenient\n+        parsing. Using model field's to_python() would reject 'false' whereas\n+        the form field accepts it.\n+        \"\"\"\n+        obj = UnorderedObject.objects.create(bool=False)\n+        UnorderedObject.objects.create(bool=True)\n+        m = admin.ModelAdmin(UnorderedObject, custom_site)\n+        m.search_fields = [\"bool__exact\"]\n+\n+        # 'false' is accepted by form field but rejected by model field.\n+        request = self.factory.get(\"/\", data={SEARCH_VAR: \"false\"})\n+        request.user = self.superuser\n+\n+        cl = m.get_changelist_instance(request)\n+        self.assertCountEqual(cl.queryset, [obj])\n+\n+    @skipUnlessDBFeature(\"supports_primitives_in_json_field\")\n+    def test_exact_lookup_validates_each_field_independently(self):\n+        \"\"\"\n+        Each field validates the search term independently without leaking\n+        converted values between fields.\n+\n+        \"3.\" is valid for IntegerField (converts to 3) but invalid for\n+        JSONField. The converted value must not leak to the JSONField check.\n+        \"\"\"\n+        # obj_int has int_field=3, should match \"3.\" via IntegerF\n... [diff truncated]",

  "content": "{\"content\":\"1: import posixpath\\n2: from collections import defaultdict\\n3: \\n4: from django.utils.safestring import mark_safe\\n5: \\n6: from .base import Node, Template, TemplateSyntaxError, TextNode, Variable, token_kwargs\\n7: from .library import Library\\n8: \\n9: register = Library()\\n10: \\n11: BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY = \\\"block_context\\\"\\n12: \\n13: \\n14: class BlockContext:\\n15:     def __init__(self):\\n16:         # Dictionary of FIFO queues.\\n17:         self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\\n18: \\n19:     def __repr__(self):\\n20:         return f\\\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\\\"\\n21: \\n22:     def add_blocks(self, blocks):\\n23:         for name, block in blocks.items():\\n24:             self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\\n25: \\n26:     def pop(self, name):\\n27:         try:\\n28:             return self.blocks[name].pop()\\n29:         except IndexError:\\n30:             return None\\n31: \\n32:     def push(self, name, block):\\n33:         self.blocks[name].append(block)\\n34: \\n35:     def get_block(self, name):\\n36:         try:\\n37:             return self.blocks[name][-1]\\n38:         except IndexError:\\n39:             return None\\n40: \\n41: \\n42: class BlockNode(Node):\\n43:     def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\\n44:         self.name = name\\n45:         self.nodelist = nodelist\\n46:         self.parent = parent\\n47: \\n48:     def __repr__(self):\\n49:         return \\\"<Block Node: %s. Contents: %r>\\\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\\n50: \\n51:     def render(self, context):\\n52:         block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\\n53:         with context.push():\\n54:             if block_context is None:\\n55:                 context[\\\"block\\\"] = self\\n56:                 result = self.nodelist.render(context)\\n57:             else:\\n58:                 push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\\n59:                 if block is None:\\n60:                     block = self\\n61:                 # Create new block so we can store context without\\n62:                 # thread-safety issues.\\n63:                 block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\\n64:                 block.context = context\\n65:                 context[\\\"block\\\"] = block\\n66:                 result = block.nodelist.render(context)\\n67:                 if push is not None:\\n68:                     block_context.push(self.name, push)\\n69:         return result\\n70: \\n71:     def super(self):\\n72:         if not hasattr(self, \\\"context\\\"):\\n73:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\n74:                 \\\"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. Did you use \\\"\\n75:                 \\\"{{ block.super }} in a base template?\\\" % self.__class__.__name__\\n76:             )\\n77:         render_context = self.context.render_context\\n78:         if (\\n79:             BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY in render_context\\n80:             and render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY].get_block(self.name) is not None\\n81:         ):\\n82:             return mark_safe(self.render(self.context))\\n83:         return \\\"\\\"\\n84: \\n85: \\n86: class ExtendsNode(Node):\\n87:     must_be_first = True\\n88:     context_key = \\\"extends_context\\\"\\n89: \\n90:     def __init__(self, nodelist, parent_name, template_dirs=None):\\n91:         self.nodelist = nodelist\\n92:         self.parent_name = parent_name\\n93:         self.template_dirs = template_dirs\\n94:         self.blocks = {n.name: n for n in nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)}\\n95: \\n96:     def __repr__(self):\\n97:         return \\\"<%s: extends %s>\\\" % (self.__class__.__name__, self.parent_name.token)\\n98: \\n99:     def find_template(self, template_name, context):\\n100:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n101:         This is a wrapper around engine.find_template(). A history is kept in\\n102:         the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\\n103:         passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\\n104:         without extending the same template twice.\\n105:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n106:         history = context.render_context.setdefault(\\n107:             self.context_key,\\n108:             [self.origin],\\n109:         )\\n110:         template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\\n111:             template_name,\\n112:             skip=history,\\n113:         )\\n114:         history.append(origin)\\n115:         return template\\n116: \\n117:     def get_parent(self, context):\\n118:         parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\\n119:         if not parent:\\n120:             error_msg = \\\"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\\\" % parent\\n121:             if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\\n122:                 error_msg += (\\n123:                     \\\" Got this from the '%s' variable.\\\" % self.parent_name.token\\n124:                 )\\n125:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\\n126:         if isinstance(parent, Template):\\n127:             # parent is a django.template.Template\\n128:             return parent\\n129:         if isinstance(getattr(parent, \\\"template\\\", None), Template):\\n130:             # parent is a django.template.backends.django.Template\\n131:             return parent.template\\n132:         return self.find_template(parent, context)\\n133: \\n134:     def render(self, context):\\n135:         compiled_parent = self.get_parent(context)\\n136: \\n137:         if BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY not in context.render_context:\\n138:             context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY] = BlockContext()\\n139:         block_context = context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY]\\n140: \\n141:         # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\\n142:         block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\\n143: \\n144:         # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\\n145:         # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\\n146:         for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\\n147:             # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\\n148:             if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\\n149:                 if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\\n150:                     blocks = {\\n151:                         n.name: n\\n152:                         for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\\n153:                     }\\n154:                     block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\\n155:                 break\\n156: \\n157:         # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\\n158:         # the same.\\n159:         with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\\n160:             return compiled_parent._render(context)\\n161: \\n162: \\n163: class IncludeNode(Node):\\n164:     context_key = \\\"__include_context\\\"\\n165: \\n166:     def __init__(\\n167:         self, template, *args, extra_context=None, isolated_context=False, **kwargs\\n168:     ):\\n169:         self.template = template\\n170:         self.extra_context = extra_context or {}\\n171:         self.isolated_context = isolated_context\\n172:         super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\\n173: \\n174:     def __repr__(self):\\n175:         return f\\\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: template={self.template!r}>\\\"\\n176: \\n177:     def render(self, context):\\n178:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n179:         Render the specified template and context. Cache the template object\\n180:         in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\\n181:         loop.\\n182:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n183:         template = self.template.resolve(context)\\n184:         # Does this quack like a Template?\\n185:         if not callable(getattr(template, \\\"render\\\", None)):\\n186:             # If not, try the cache and select_template().\\n187:             template_name = template or ()\\n188:             if isinstance(template_name, str):\\n189:                 template_name = (\\n190:                     construct_relative_path(\\n191:                         self.origin.template_name,\\n192:                         template_name,\\n193:                     ),\\n194:                 )\\n195:             else:\\n196:                 template_name = tuple(template_name)\\n197:             cache = context.render_context.dicts[0].setdefault(self, {})\\n198:             template = cache.get(template_name)\\n199:             if template is None:\\n200:                 template = context.template.engine.select_template(template_name)\\n201:                 cache[template_name] = template\\n202:         # Use the base.Template of a backends.django.Template.\\n203:         elif hasattr(template, \\\"template\\\"):\\n204:             template = template.template\\n205:         values = {\\n206:             name: var.resolve(context) for name, var in self.extra_context.items()\\n207:         }\\n208:         if self.isolated_context:\\n209:             return template.render(context.new(values))\\n210:         with context.push(**values):\\n211:             return template.render(context)\\n212: \\n213: \\n214: @register.tag(\\\"block\\\")\\n215: def do_block(parser, token):\\n216:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n217:     Define a block that can be overridden by child templates.\\n218:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n219:     # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\\n220:     # variable as arguments.\\n221:     bits = token.contents.split()\\n222:     if len(bits) != 2:\\n223:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\\\" % bits[0])\\n224:     block_name = bits[1]\\n225:     # Keep track of the names of BlockNodes found in this template, so we can\\n226:     # check for duplication.\\n227:     try:\\n228:         if block_name in parser.__loaded_blocks:\\n229:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\n230:                 \\\"'%s' tag with name '%s' appears more than once\\\" % (bits[0], block_name)\\n231:             )\\n232:         parser.__loaded_blocks.append(block_name)\\n233:     except AttributeError:  # parser.__loaded_blocks isn't a list yet\\n234:         parser.__loaded_blocks = [block_name]\\n235:     nodelist = parser.parse((\\\"endblock\\\",))\\n236: \\n237:     # This check is kept for backwards-compatibility. See #3100.\\n238:     endblock = parser.next_token()\\n239:     acceptable_endblocks = (\\\"endblock\\\", \\\"endblock %s\\\" % block_name)\\n240:     if endblock.contents not in acceptable_endblocks:\\n241:         parser.invalid_block_tag(endblock, \\\"endblock\\\", acceptable_endblocks)\\n242: \\n243:     return BlockNode(block_name, nodelist)\\n244: \\n245: \\n246: def construct_relative_path(\\n247:     current_template_name,\\n248:     relative_name,\\n249:     allow_recursion=False,\\n250: ):\\n251:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n252:     Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\\n253:     name based on the current_template_name.\\n254:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n255:     new_name = relative_name.strip(\\\"'\\\\\\\"\\\")\\n256:     if not new_name.startswith((\\\"./\\\", \\\"../\\\")):\\n257:         # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\\n258:         # relative path.\\n259:         return relative_name\\n260: \\n261:     if current_template_name is None:\\n262:         # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\\n263:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\n264: posixpath.normpath(\\n269:         posixpath.join(\\n270:             posixpath.dirname(current_template_name.lstrip(\\\"/\\\")),\\n271:             new_name,\\n272:         )\\n273:     )\\n274: '%s' is in.\\\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\\n278:         )\\n279:     if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\\\"/\\\") == new_name:\\n280:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\n281:             \\\"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \\\"\\n282:             \\\"same template in which the tag appears.\\\"\\n283:             % (relative_name, current_template_name)\\n284:         )\\n285:     has_quotes = (\\n286:         relative_name.startswith(('\\\"', \\\"'\\\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\\n287:     )\\n288:     return f'\\\"{new_name}\\\"' if has_quotes else new_name\\n289: \\n290: \\n291: @register.tag(\\\"extends\\\")\\n292: def do_extends(parser, token):\\n293:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n294:     Signal that this template extends a parent template.\\n295: \\n296:     This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \\\"base\\\" %}`` (with quotes)\\n297:     uses the literal value \\\"base\\\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\\n298:     or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\\n299:     name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\\n300:     the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a Template object).\\n301:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n302:     bits = token.split_contents()\\n303:     if len(bits) != 2:\\n304: parser.compile_filter(bits[1])\\n307:     nodelist = parser.parse()\\n308:     if nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(ExtendsNode):\\n309:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\n310:             \\\"'%s' cannot appear more than once in the same template\\\" % bits[0]\\n311:         )\\n312:     return ExtendsNode(nodelist, parent_name)\\n313: \\n314: \\n315: @register.tag(\\\"include\\\")\\n316: def do_include(parser, token):\\n317:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n318:     Load a template and render it with the current context. You can pass\\n319:     additional context using keyword arguments.\\n320: \\n321:     Example::\\n322: \\n323:         {% include \\\"foo/some_include\\\" %}\\n324:         {% include \\\"foo/some_include\\\" with bar=\\\"BAZZ!\\\" baz=\\\"BING!\\\" %}\\n325: \\n326:     Use the ``only`` argument to exclude the current context when rendering\\n327:     the included template::\\n328: \\n329:         {% include \\\"foo/some_include\\\" only %}\\n330:         {% include \\\"foo/some_include\\\" with bar=\\\"1\\\" only %}\\n331:     \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n332:     bits = token.split_contents()\\n333:     if len(bits) < 2:\\n334:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\n335:             \\\"%r tag takes at least one argument: the name of the template to \\\"\\n336:             \\\"be included.\\\" % bits[0]\\n337:         )\\n338:     options = {}\\n339:     remaining_bits = bits[2:]\\n340:     while remaining_bits:\\n341:         option = remaining_bits.pop(0)\\n342:         if option in options:\\n343:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\n344:                 \\\"The %r option was specified more than once.\\\" % option\\n345:             )\\n346:         if option == \\\"with\\\":\\n347:             value = token_kwargs(remaining_bits, parser, support_legacy=False)\\n348:             if not value:\\n349:                 raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\n350:                     '\\\"with\\\" in %r tag needs at least one keyword argument.' % bits[0]\\n351:                 )\\n352:         elif option == \\\"only\\\":\\n353:             value = True\\n354:         else:\\n355:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(\\n356:                 \\\"Unknown argument for %r tag: %r.\\\" % (bits[0], option)\\n357:             )\\n358:         options[option] = value\\n359:     isolated_context = options.get(\\\"only\\\", False)\\n360:     namemap = options.get(\\\"with\\\", {})\\n361:     bits[1] = construct_relative_path(\\n362:         parser.origin.template_name,\\n363:         bits[1],\\n364:         allow_recursion=True,\\n365:     )\\n366:     return IncludeNode(\\n367:         parser.compile_filter(bits[1]),\\n368: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/template/loader_tags.py\"}",

    "content": "1: import posixpath\n2: from collections import defaultdict\n3: \n4: from django.utils.safestring import mark_safe\n5: \n6: from .base import Node, Template, TemplateSyntaxError, TextNode, Variable, token_kwargs\n7: from .library import Library\n8: \n9: register = Library()\n10: \n11: BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY = \"block_context\"\n12: \n13: \n14: class BlockContext:\n15:     def __init__(self):\n16:         # Dictionary of FIFO queues.\n17:         self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n18: \n19:     def __repr__(self):\n20:         return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n21: \n22:     def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n23:         for name, block in blocks.items():\n24:             self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n25: \n26:     def pop(self, name):\n27:         try:\n28:             return self.blocks[name].pop()\n29:         except IndexError:\n30:             return None\n31: \n32:     def push(self, name, block):\n33:         self.blocks[name].append(block)\n34: \n35:     def get_block(self, name):\n36:         try:\n37:             return self.blocks[name][-1]\n38:         except IndexError:\n39:             return None\n40: \n41: \n42: class BlockNode(Node):\n43:     def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\n44:         self.name = name\n45:         self.nodelist = nodelist\n46:         self.parent = parent\n47: \n48:     def __repr__(self):\n49:         return \"<Block Node: %s. Contents: %r>\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\n50: \n51:     def render(self, context):\n52:         block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n53:         with context.push():\n54:             if block_context is None:\n55:                 context[\"block\"] = self\n56:                 result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n57:             else:\n58:                 push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n59:                 if block is None:\n60:                     block = self\n61:                 # Create new block so we can store context without\n62:                 # thread-safety issues.\n63:                 block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n64:                 block.context = context\n65:                 context[\"block\"] = block\n66:                 result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n67:                 if push is not None:\n68:                     block_context.push(self.name, push)\n69:         return result\n70: \n71:     def super(self):\n72:         if not hasattr(self, \"context\"):\n73:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n74:                 \"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. Did you use \"\n75:                 \"{{ block.super }} in a base template?\" % self.__class__.__name__\n76:             )\n77:         render_context = self.context.render_context\n78:         if (\n79:             BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY in render_context\n80:             and render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY].get_block(self.name) is not None\n81:         ):\n82:             return mark_safe(self.render(self.context))\n83:         return \"\"\n84: \n85: \n86: class ExtendsNode(Node):\n87:     must_be_first = True\n88:     context_key = \"extends_context\"\n89: \n90:     def __init__(self, nodelist, parent_name, template_dirs=None):\n91:         self.nodelist = nodelist\n92:         self.parent_name = parent_name\n93:         self.template_dirs = template_dirs\n94:         self.blocks = {n.name: n for n in nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)}\n95: \n96:     def __repr__(self):\n97:         return \"<%s: extends %s>\" % (self.__class__.__name__, self.parent_name.token)\n98: \n99:     def find_template(self, template_name, context):\n100:         \"\"\"\n101:         This is a wrapper around engine.find_template(). A history is kept in\n102:         the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n103:         passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n104:         without extending the same template twice.\n105:         \"\"\"\n106:         history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n107:             self.context_key,\n108:             [self.origin],\n109:         )\n110:         template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n111:             template_name,\n112:             skip=history,\n113:         )\n114:         history.append(origin)\n115:         return template\n116: \n117:     def get_parent(self, context):\n118:         parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n119:         if not parent:\n120:             error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n121:             if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n122:                 error_msg += (\n123:                     \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n124:                 )\n125:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n126:         if isinstance(parent, Template):\n127:             # parent is a django.template.Template\n128:             return parent\n129:         if isinstance(getattr(parent, \"template\", None), Template):\n130:             # parent is a django.template.backends.django.Template\n131:             return parent.template\n132:         return self.find_template(parent, context)\n133: \n134:     def render(self, context):\n135:         compiled_parent = self.get_parent(context)\n136: \n137:         if BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY not in context.render_context:\n138:             context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY] = BlockContext()\n139:         block_context = context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY]\n140: \n141:         # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n142:         block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n143: \n144:         # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n145:         # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n146:         for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n147:             # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n148:             if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n149:                 if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n150:                     blocks = {\n151:                         n.name: n\n152:                         for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n153:                     }\n154:                     block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n155:                 break\n156: \n157:         # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n158:         # the same.\n159:         with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n160:             return compiled_parent._render(context)\n161: \n162: \n163: class IncludeNode(Node):\n164:     context_key = \"__include_context\"\n165: \n166:     def __init__(\n167:         self, template, *args, extra_context=None, isolated_context=False, **kwargs\n168:     ):\n169:         self.template = template\n170:         self.extra_context = extra_context or {}\n171:         self.isolated_context = isolated_context\n172:         super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\n173: \n174:     def __repr__(self):\n175:         return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: template={self.template!r}>\"\n176: \n177:     def render(self, context):\n178:         \"\"\"\n179:         Render the specified template and context. Cache the template object\n180:         in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\n181:         loop.\n182:         \"\"\"\n183:         template = self.template.resolve(context)\n184:         # Does this quack like a Template?\n185:         if not callable(getattr(template, \"render\", None)):\n186:             # If not, try the cache and select_template().\n187:             template_name = template or ()\n188:             if isinstance(template_name, str):\n189:                 template_name = (\n190:                     construct_relative_path(\n191:                         self.origin.template_name,\n192:                         template_name,\n193:                     ),\n194:                 )\n195:             else:\n196:                 template_name = tuple(template_name)\n197:             cache = context.render_context.dicts[0].setdefault(self, {})\n198:             template = cache.get(template_name)\n199:             if template is None:\n200:                 template = context.template.engine.select_template(template_name)\n201:                 cache[template_name] = template\n202:         # Use the base.Template of a backends.django.Template.\n203:         elif hasattr(template, \"template\"):\n204:             template = template.template\n205:         values = {\n206:             name: var.resolve(context) for name, var in self.extra_context.items()\n207:         }\n208:         if self.isolated_context:\n209:             return template.render(context.new(values))\n210:         with context.push(**values):\n211:             return template.render(context)\n212: \n213: \n214: @register.tag(\"block\")\n215: def do_block(parser, token):\n216:     \"\"\"\n217:     Define a block that can be overridden by child templates.\n218:     \"\"\"\n219:     # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\n220:     # variable as arguments.\n221:     bits = token.contents.split()\n222:     if len(bits) != 2:\n223:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\" % bits[0])\n224:     block_name = bits[1]\n225:     # Keep track of the names of BlockNodes found in this template, so we can\n226:     # check for duplication.\n227:     try:\n228:         if block_name in parser.__loaded_blocks:\n229:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n230:                 \"'%s' tag with name '%s' appears more than once\" % (bits[0], block_name)\n231:             )\n232:         parser.__loaded_blocks.append(block_name)\n233:     except AttributeError:  # parser.__loaded_blocks isn't a list yet\n234:         parser.__loaded_blocks = [block_name]\n235:     nodelist = parser.parse((\"endblock\",))\n236: \n237:     # This check is kept for backwards-compatibility. See #3100.\n238:     endblock = parser.next_token()\n239:     acceptable_endblocks = (\"endblock\", \"endblock %s\" % block_name)\n240:     if endblock.contents not in acceptable_endblocks:\n241:         parser.invalid_block_tag(endblock, \"endblock\", acceptable_endblocks)\n242: \n243:     return BlockNode(block_name, nodelist)\n244: \n245: \n246: def construct_relative_path(\n247:     current_template_name,\n248:     relative_name,\n249:     allow_recursion=False,\n250: ):\n251:     \"\"\"\n252:     Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n253:     name based on the current_template_name.\n254:     \"\"\"\n255:     new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n256:     if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n257:         # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n258:         # relative path.\n259:         return relative_name\n260: \n261:     if current_template_name is None:\n262:         # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n263:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n264:             f\"The relative path {relative_name} cannot be evaluated due to \"\n265:             \"an unknown template origin.\"\n266:         )\n267: \n268:     new_name = posixpath.normpath(\n269:         posixpath.join(\n270:             posixpath.dirname(current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\")),\n271:             new_name,\n272:         )\n273:     )\n274:     if new_name.startswith(\"../\"):\n275:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n276:             \"The relative path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n277:             \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n278:         )\n279:     if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n280:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n281:             \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n282:             \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n283:             % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n284:         )\n285:     has_quotes = (\n286:         relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n287:     )\n288:     return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n289: \n290: \n291: @register.tag(\"extends\")\n292: def do_extends(parser, token):\n293:     \"\"\"\n294:     Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n295: \n296:     This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n297:     uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n298:     or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n299:     name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n300:     the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a Template object).\n301:     \"\"\"\n302:     bits = token.split_contents()\n303:     if len(bits) != 2:\n304:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' takes one argument\" % bits[0])\n305:     bits[1] = construct_relative_path(parser.origin.template_name, bits[1])\n306:     parent_name = parser.compile_filter(bits[1])\n307:     nodelist = parser.parse()\n308:     if nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(ExtendsNode):\n309:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n310:             \"'%s' cannot appear more than once in the same template\" % bits[0]\n311:         )\n312:     return ExtendsNode(nodelist, parent_name)\n313: \n314: \n315: @register.tag(\"include\")\n316: def do_include(parser, token):\n317:     \"\"\"\n318:     Load a template and render it with the current context. You can pass\n319:     additional context using keyword arguments.\n320: \n321:     Example::\n322: \n323:         {% include \"foo/some_include\" %}\n324:         {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"BAZZ!\" baz=\"BING!\" %}\n325: \n326:     Use the ``only`` argument to exclude the current context when rendering\n327:     the included template::\n328: \n329:         {% include \"foo/some_include\" only %}\n330:         {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"1\" only %}\n331:     \"\"\"\n332:     bits = token.split_contents()\n333:     if len(bits) < 2:\n334:         raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n335:             \"%r tag takes at least one argument: the name of the template to \"\n336:             \"be included.\" % bits[0]\n337:         )\n338:     options = {}\n339:     remaining_bits = bits[2:]\n340:     while remaining_bits:\n341:         option = remaining_bits.pop(0)\n342:         if option in options:\n343:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n344:                 \"The %r option was specified more than once.\" % option\n345:             )\n346:         if option == \"with\":\n347:             value = token_kwargs(remaining_bits, parser, support_legacy=False)\n348:             if not value:\n349:                 raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n350:                     '\"with\" in %r tag needs at least one keyword argument.' % bits[0]\n351:                 )\n352:         elif option == \"only\":\n353:             value = True\n354:         else:\n355:             raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n356:                 \"Unknown argument for %r tag: %r.\" % (bits[0], option)\n357:             )\n358:         options[option] = value\n359:     isolated_context = options.get(\"only\", False)\n360:     namemap = options.get(\"with\", {})\n361:     bits[1] = construct_relative_path(\n362:         parser.origin.template_name,\n363:         bits[1],\n364:         allow_recursion=True,\n365:     )\n366:     return IncludeNode(\n367:         parser.compile_filter(bits[1]),\n368:         extra_context=namemap,\n369:         isolated_context=isolated_context,\n370:     )\n371: ",

32. mcp__sourcegraph__sg_read_file @ -

{
  "endLine": 100,
  "path": "django/template/base.py",
  "repo": "github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c",
  "startLine": 1
}
Output
{
  "content": "{\"content\":\"1: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n2: This is the Django template system.\\n3: \\n4: How it works:\\n5: \\n6: The Lexer.tokenize() method converts a template string (i.e., a string\\n7: containing markup with custom template tags) to tokens, which can be either\\n8: plain text (TokenType.TEXT), variables (TokenType.VAR), or block statements\\n9: (TokenType.BLOCK).\\n10: \\n11: The Parser() class takes a list of tokens in its constructor, and its parse()\\n12: method returns a compiled template -- which is, under the hood, a list of\\n13: Node objects.\\n14: \\n15: Each Node is responsible for creating some sort of output -- e.g. simple text\\n16: (TextNode), variable values in a given context (VariableNode), results of basic\\n17: logic (IfNode), results of looping (ForNode), or anything else. The core Node\\n18: types are TextNode, VariableNode, IfNode and ForNode, but plugin modules can\\n19: define their own custom node types.\\n20: \\n21: Each Node has a render() method, which takes a Context and returns a string of\\n22: the rendered node. For example, the render() method of a Variable Node returns\\n23: the variable's value as a string. The render() method of a ForNode returns the\\n24: rendered output of whatever was inside the loop, recursively.\\n25: \\n26: The Template class is a convenient wrapper that takes care of template\\n27: compilation and rendering.\\n28: \\n29: Usage:\\n30: \\n31: The only thing you should ever use directly in this file is the Template class.\\n32: Create a compiled template object with a template_string, then call render()\\n33: with a context. In the compilation stage, the TemplateSyntaxError exception\\n34: will be raised if the template doesn't have proper syntax.\\n35: \\n36: Sample code:\\n37: \\n38: >>> from django import template\\n39: >>> s = '<html>{% if test %}<h1>{{ varvalue }}</h1>{% endif %}</html>'\\n40: >>> t = template.Template(s)\\n41: \\n42: (t is now a compiled template, and its render() method can be called multiple\\n43: times with multiple contexts)\\n44: \\n45: >>> c = template.Context({'test':True, 'varvalue': 'Hello'})\\n46: >>> t.render(c)\\n47: '<html><h1>Hello</h1></html>'\\n48: >>> c = template.Context({'test':False, 'varvalue': 'Hello'})\\n49: >>> t.render(c)\\n50: '<html></html>'\\n51: \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n52: \\n53: import inspect\\n54: import logging\\n55: import re\\n56: import warnings\\n57: from enum import Enum\\n58: \\n59: from django.template.context import BaseContext\\n60: from django.utils.deprecation import django_file_prefixes\\n61: from django.utils.formats import localize\\n62: from django.utils.html import conditional_escape\\n63: from django.utils.inspect import lazy_annotations\\n64: from django.utils.regex_helper import _lazy_re_compile\\n65: from django.utils.safestring import SafeData, SafeString, mark_safe\\n66: from django.utils.text import get_text_list, smart_split, unescape_string_literal\\n67: from django.utils.timezone import template_localtime\\n68: from django.utils.translation import gettext_lazy, pgettext_lazy\\n69: \\n70: from .exceptions import TemplateSyntaxError\\n71: \\n72: # template syntax constants\\n73: FILTER_SEPARATOR = \\\"|\\\"\\n74: FILTER_ARGUMENT_SEPARATOR = \\\":\\\"\\n75: VARIABLE_ATTRIBUTE_SEPARATOR = \\\".\\\"\\n76: BLOCK_TAG_START = \\\"{%\\\"\\n77: BLOCK_TAG_END = \\\"%}\\\"\\n78: VARIABLE_TAG_START = \\\"{{\\\"\\n79: VARIABLE_TAG_END = \\\"}}\\\"\\n80: COMMENT_TAG_START = \\\"{#\\\"\\n81: COMMENT_TAG_END = \\\"#}\\\"\\n82: SINGLE_BRACE_START = \\\"{\\\"\\n83: SINGLE_BRACE_END = \\\"}\\\"\\n84: \\n85: # what to report as the origin for templates that come from non-loader sources\\n86: # (e.g. strings)\\n87: UNKNOWN_SOURCE = \\\"<unknown source>\\\"\\n88: \\n89: # Match BLOCK_TAG_*, VARIABLE_TAG_*, and COMMENT_TAG_* tags and capture the\\n90: # entire tag, including start/end delimiters. Using re.compile() is faster\\n91: # than instantiating SimpleLazyObject with _lazy_re_compile().\\n92: tag_re = re.compile(r\\\"({%.*?%}|{{.*?}}|{#.*?#})\\\")\\n93: \\n94: logger = logging.getLogger(\\\"django.template\\\")\\n95: \\n96: \\n97: class TokenType(Enum):\\n98:     TEXT = 0\\n99:     VAR = 1\\n100:     BLOCK = 2\\n101: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/template/base.py%3FL1-100\"}",
  "structuredContent": {
    "content": "1: \"\"\"\n2: This is the Django template system.\n3: \n4: How it works:\n5: \n6: The Lexer.tokenize() method converts a template string (i.e., a string\n7: containing markup with custom template tags) to tokens, which can be either\n8: plain text (TokenType.TEXT), variables (TokenType.VAR), or block statements\n9: (TokenType.BLOCK).\n10: \n11: The Parser() class takes a list of tokens in its constructor, and its parse()\n12: method returns a compiled template -- which is, under the hood, a list of\n13: Node objects.\n14: \n15: Each Node is responsible for creating some sort of output -- e.g. simple text\n16: (TextNode), variable values in a given context (VariableNode), results of basic\n17: logic (IfNode), results of looping (ForNode), or anything else. The core Node\n18: types are TextNode, VariableNode, IfNode and ForNode, but plugin modules can\n19: define their own custom node types.\n20: \n21: Each Node has a render() method, which takes a Context and returns a string of\n22: the rendered node. For example, the render() method of a Variable Node returns\n23: the variable's value as a string. The render() method of a ForNode returns the\n24: rendered output of whatever was inside the loop, recursively.\n25: \n26: The Template class is a convenient wrapper that takes care of template\n27: compilation and rendering.\n28: \n29: Usage:\n30: \n31: The only thing you should ever use directly in this file is the Template class.\n32: Create a compiled template object with a template_string, then call render()\n33: with a context. In the compilation stage, the TemplateSyntaxError exception\n34: will be raised if the template doesn't have proper syntax.\n35: \n36: Sample code:\n37: \n38: >>> from django import template\n39: >>> s = '<html>{% if test %}<h1>{{ varvalue }}</h1>{% endif %}</html>'\n40: >>> t = template.Template(s)\n41: \n42: (t is now a compiled template, and its render() method can be called multiple\n43: times with multiple contexts)\n44: \n45: >>> c = template.Context({'test':True, 'varvalue': 'Hello'})\n46: >>> t.render(c)\n47: '<html><h1>Hello</h1></html>'\n48: >>> c = template.Context({'test':False, 'varvalue': 'Hello'})\n49: >>> t.render(c)\n50: '<html></html>'\n51: \"\"\"\n52: \n53: import inspect\n54: import logging\n55: import re\n56: import warnings\n57: from enum import Enum\n58: \n59: from django.template.context import BaseContext\n60: from django.utils.deprecation import django_file_prefixes\n61: from django.utils.formats import localize\n62: from django.utils.html import conditional_escape\n63: from django.utils.inspect import lazy_annotations\n64: from django.utils.regex_helper import _lazy_re_compile\n65: from django.utils.safestring import SafeData, SafeString, mark_safe\n66: from django.utils.text import get_text_list, smart_split, unescape_string_literal\n67: from django.utils.timezone import template_localtime\n68: from django.utils.translation import gettext_lazy, pgettext_lazy\n69: \n70: from .exceptions import TemplateSyntaxError\n71: \n72: # template syntax constants\n73: FILTER_SEPARATOR = \"|\"\n74: FILTER_ARGUMENT_SEPARATOR = \":\"\n75: VARIABLE_ATTRIBUTE_SEPARATOR = \".\"\n76: BLOCK_TAG_START = \"{%\"\n77: BLOCK_TAG_END = \"%}\"\n78: VARIABLE_TAG_START = \"{{\"\n79: VARIABLE_TAG_END = \"}}\"\n80: COMMENT_TAG_START = \"{#\"\n81: COMMENT_TAG_END = \"#}\"\n82: SINGLE_BRACE_START = \"{\"\n83: SINGLE_BRACE_END = \"}\"\n84: \n85: # what to report as the origin for templates that come from non-loader sources\n86: # (e.g. strings)\n87: UNKNOWN_SOURCE = \"<unknown source>\"\n88: \n89: # Match BLOCK_TAG_*, VARIABLE_TAG_*, and COMMENT_TAG_* tags and capture the\n90: # entire tag, including start/end delimiters. Using re.compile() is faster\n91: # than instantiating SimpleLazyObject with _lazy_re_compile().\n92: tag_re = re.compile(r\"({%.*?%}|{{.*?}}|{#.*?#})\")\n93: \n94: logger = logging.getLogger(\"django.template\")\n95: \n96: \n97: class TokenType(Enum):\n98:     TEXT = 0\n99:     VAR = 1\n100:     BLOCK = 2\n101: ",
    "url": "https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/template/base.py%3FL1-100"
  }

33. Bash @ -
Input
{
  "command": "head -100 /home/claude/.local/lib/python3.11/site-packages/django/template/base.py",
  "description": "Read first 100 lines of installed base.py"
}
Output
{
  "interrupted": false,
  "isImage": false,
  "noOutputExpected": false,
  "stderr": "",
  "stdout": "\"\"\"\nThis is the Django template system.\n\nHow it works:\n\nThe Lexer.tokenize() method converts a template string (i.e., a string\ncontaining markup with custom template tags) to tokens, which can be either\nplain text (TokenType.TEXT), variables (TokenType.VAR), or block statements\n(TokenType.BLOCK).\n\nThe Parser() class takes a list of tokens in its constructor, and its parse()\nmethod returns a compiled template -- which is, under the hood, a list of\nNode objects.\n\nEach Node is responsible for creating some sort of output -- e.g. simple text\n(TextNode), variable values in a given context (VariableNode), results of basic\nlogic (IfNode), results of looping (ForNode), or anything else. The core Node\ntypes are TextNode, VariableNode, IfNode and ForNode, but plugin modules can\ndefine their own custom node types.\n\nEach Node has a render() method, which takes a Context and returns a string of\nthe rendered node. For example, the render() method of a Variable Node returns\nthe variable's value as a string. The render() method of a ForNode returns the\nrendered output of whatever was inside the loop, recursively.\n\nThe Template class is a convenient wrapper that takes care of template\ncompilation and rendering.\n\nUsage:\n\nThe only thing you should ever use directly in this file is the Template class.\nCreate a compiled template object with a template_string, then call render()\nwith a context. In the compilation stage, the TemplateSyntaxError exception\nwill be raised if the template doesn't have proper syntax.\n\nSample code:\n\n>>> from django import template\n>>> s = '<html>{% if test %}<h1>{{ varvalue }}</h1>{% endif %}</html>'\n>>> t = template.Template(s)\n\n(t is now a compiled template, and its render() method can be called multiple\ntimes with multiple contexts)\n\n>>> c = template.Context({'test':True, 'varvalue': 'Hello'})\n>>> t.render(c)\n'<html><h1>Hello</h1></html>'\n>>> c = template.Context({'test':False, 'varvalue': 'Hello'})\n>>> t.render(c)\n'<html></html>'\n\"\"\"\n\nimport inspect\nimport logging\nimport re\nfrom enum import Enum\n\nfrom django.template.context import BaseContext\nfrom django.utils.formats import localize\nfrom django.utils.html import conditional_escape\nfrom django.utils.inspect import lazy_annotations, signature\nfrom django.utils.regex_helper import _lazy_re_compile\nfrom django.utils.safestring import SafeData, SafeString, mark_safe\nfrom django.utils.text import get_text_list, smart_split, unescape_string_literal\nfrom django.utils.timezone import template_localtime\nfrom django.utils.translation import gettext_lazy, pgettext_lazy\n\nfrom .exceptions import TemplateSyntaxError\n\n# template syntax constants\nFILTER_SEPARATOR = \"|\"\nFILTER_ARGUMENT_SEPARATOR = \":\"\nVARIABLE_ATTRIBUTE_SEPARATOR = \".\"\nBLOCK_TAG_START = \"{%\"\nBLOCK_TAG_END = \"%}\"\nVARIABLE_TAG_START = \"{{\"\nVARIABLE_TAG_END = \"}}\"\nCOMMENT_TAG_START = \"{#\"\nCOMMENT_TAG_END = \"#}\"\nSINGLE_BRACE_START = \"{\"\nSINGLE_BRACE_END = \"}\"\n\n# what to report as the origin for templates that come from non-loader sources\n# (e.g. strings)\nUNKNOWN_SOURCE = \"<unknown source>\"\n\n# Match BLOCK_TAG_*, VARIABLE_TAG_*, and COMMENT_TAG_* tags and capture the\n# entire tag, including start/end delimiters. Using re.compile() is faster\n# than instantiating SimpleLazyObject with _lazy_re_compile().\ntag_re = re.compile(r\"({%.*?%}|{{.*?}}|{#.*?#})\")\n\nlogger = logging.getLogger(\"django.template\")\n\n\nclass TokenType(Enum):\n    TEXT = 0\n    VAR = 1\n    BLOCK = 2\n    COMMENT = 3"
}

list index out of range\\n985:                             ValueError,  # invalid literal for int()\\n986:                             KeyError,  # current is a dict without `int(bit)` key\\n987:                             TypeError,\\n988:                         ):  # unsubscriptable object\\n989:                             raise VariableDoesNotExist(\\n990:                                 \\\"Failed lookup for key [%s] in %r\\\",\\n991:                                 (bit, current),\\n992:                             )  # missing attribute\\n993:                 if callable(current):\\n994:                     if getattr(current, \\\"do_not_call_in_templates\\\", False):\\n995:                         pass\\n996:                     elif getattr(current, \\\"alters_data\\\", False):\\n997:                         current = context.template.engine.string_if_invalid\\n998:                     else:\\n999:                         try:  # method call (assuming no args required)\\n1000:                             current = current()\\n1001:                         except TypeError:\\n1002:                             try:\\n1003:                                 signature = inspect.signature(current)\\n1004:                             except ValueError:  # No signature found.\\n1005:                                 current = context.template.engine.string_if_invalid\\n1006:                             else:\\n1007:                                 try:\\n1008:                                     signature.bind()\\n1009:                                 except TypeError:  # Arguments *were* required.\\n1010:                                     # Invalid method call.\\n1011:                                     current = context.template.engine.string_if_invalid\\n1012:                                 else:\\n1013:                                     raise\\n1014:         except Exception as e:\\n1015:             template_name = getattr(context, \\\"template_name\\\", None) or \\\"unknown\\\"\\n1016:             logger.debug(\\n1017:                 \\\"Exception while resolving variable '%s' in template '%s'.\\\",\\n1018:                 bit,\\n1019:                 template_name,\\n1020:                 exc_info=True,\\n1021:             )\\n1022: \\n1023:             if getattr(e, \\\"silent_variable_failure\\\", False):\\n1024:                 current = context.template.engine.string_if_invalid\\n1025:             else:\\n1026:                 raise\\n1027: \\n1028:         return current\\n1029: \\n1030: \\n1031: class Node:\\n1032:     # Set this to True for nodes that must be first in the template (although\\n1033:     # they can be preceded by text nodes.\\n1034:     must_be_first = False\\n1035:     child_nodelists = (\\\"nodelist\\\",)\\n1036:     token = None\\n1037: \\n1038:     def render(self, context):\\n1039:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n1040:         Return the node rendered as a string.\\n1041:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n1042:         pass\\n1043: \\n1044:     def render_annotated(self, context):\\n1045:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n1046:         Render the node. If debug is True and an exception occurs during\\n1047:         rendering, the exception is annotated with contextual line information\\n1048:         where it occurred in the template. For internal usage this method is\\n1049:         preferred over using the render method directly.\\n1050:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n1051:         try:\\n1052:             return self.render(context)\\n1053:         except Exception as e:\\n1054:             if context.template.engine.debug:\\n1055:                 # Store the actual node that caused the exception.\\n1056:                 if not hasattr(e, \\\"_culprit_node\\\"):\\n1057:                     e._culprit_node = self\\n1058:                 if (\\n1059:                     not hasattr(e, \\\"template_debug\\\")\\n1060:                     and context.render_context.template.origin == e._culprit_node.origin\\n1061:                 ):\\n1062:                     e.template_debug = (\\n1063:                         context.render_context.template.get_exception_info(\\n1064:                             e,\\n1065:                             e._culprit_node.token,\\n1066:                         )\\n1067:                     )\\n1068:             raise\\n1069: \\n1070:     def get_nodes_by_type(self, nodetype):\\n1071:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n1072:         Return a list of all nodes (within this node and its nodelist)\\n1073:         of the given type\\n1074:         \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n1075:         nodes = []\\n1076:         if isinstance(self, nodetype):\\n1077:             nodes.append(self)\\n1078:         for attr in self.child_nodelists:\\n1079:             nodelist = getattr(self, attr, None)\\n1080:             if nodelist:\\n1081:                 nodes.extend(nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(nodetype))\\n1082:         return nodes\\n1083: \\n1084: \\n1085: class NodeList(list):\\n1086:     # Set to True the first time a non-TextNode is inserted by\\n1087:     # extend_nodelist().\\n1088:     contains_nontext = False\\n1089: \\n1090:     def render(self, context):\\n1091:         return SafeString(\\\"\\\".join([node.render_annotated(context) for node in self]))\\n1092: \\n1093:     def get_nodes_by_type(self, nodetype):\\n1094:         \\\"Return a list of all nodes of the given type\\\"\\n1095:         nodes = []\\n1096:         for node in self:\\n1097:             nodes.extend(node.get_nodes_by_type(nodetype))\\n1098:         return nodes\\n1099: \\n1100: \\n1101: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/django--674eda1c@674eda1c03a3187905f48afee0f15226aa62fdf3/-/blob/django/template/base.py%3FL980-1100\"}",
